raw_text: Weekly engineering all-hands notes — April 14. Attendees: Alice Chen (PM), Bob Rivera (Eng Lead), Carol Kim (Design), David Park (Infra), Eva Sharma (Backend), Frank Liu (iOS), Grace Wong (Web), Henry Adams (QA). Alice opened with Q2 priorities: launch the new search feature by May 31st, complete the GDPR audit by June 15th, and hit 99.9% uptime SLA. Bob noted the API v2 is on schedule for May 15th; the main risk is the database migration that Frank and Grace are coordinating.

Slack thread #infra — David Park: the Postgres upgrade to version 16 is scheduled for Saturday 2am–4am UTC. Impact: all services will be in read-only mode during the maintenance window. Please plan your deploys accordingly. Eva Sharma asked if the search indexes need a rebuild after upgrade. David confirmed yes — full HNSW index rebuild is estimated at 90 minutes on production data, so the window extends to 5:30am. Carol Kim flagged this conflicts with the APAC team demo at 6am; David will coordinate with ops to check if we can start earlier.

Document: Q2 OKRs — Platform Team. Objective 1: Ship reliable search. KR1.1: p95 search latency < 200ms by June 1st (owner: Eva Sharma). KR1.2: Search recall@10 > 0.85 on the gold eval set (owner: Henry Adams). Objective 2: Improve developer experience. KR2.1: Onboarding time < 3 days (owner: Alice Chen). KR2.2: CI/CD pipeline build time < 8 minutes (owner: David Park). Objective 3: Maintain stability. KR3.1: 99.9% uptime across all production services (owner: Bob Rivera). Review cadence: monthly OKR check-ins, weekly status in all-hands.

1:1 — Alice and Henry Adams. Henry reported that QA automation coverage is now at 78%; target is 85% by end of quarter. Three test suites are failing flakily in CI: the search integration tests, the auth token refresh tests, and the billing reconciliation tests. Henry owns fixing the search and auth flakes; Grace Wong owns billing since she wrote that module. Alice approved two additional days of QA infrastructure work for Henry to address the flake root causes.

Slack #backend — Eva Sharma: shipped the new query planner for full-text search. Benchmarks on staging: p50 latency dropped from 180ms to 65ms, p95 from 420ms to 140ms. The improvement comes from pushing the workspace_id filter before the vector scan rather than after. Bob Rivera asked for a writeup in the architecture doc. Eva to add a section to the search design doc by Friday. Henry Adams will run the recall benchmark suite against the new planner tomorrow morning.

Document: Search Architecture (updated by Eva Sharma). The hybrid search pipeline uses three channels: dense vector search via pgvector HNSW index, full-text search via Postgres tsvector with GIN index, and exact entity name matching. Results are fused via reciprocal rank fusion (RRF). The workspace_id predicate is pushed to the scan level on all three channels to leverage partial indexes and enforce tenant isolation. Query planning order: (1) workspace predicate push, (2) access level filter, (3) vector/text scan, (4) RRF fusion, (5) cursor pagination. The HNSW index uses cosine distance with ef_search=100.

Meeting: Design system handoff — Carol Kim and Grace Wong. The new component library is ready for integration. Carol walked through the 12 updated components: Button, Input, Select, Checkbox, Radio, Modal, Toast, Table, Badge, Avatar, Sidebar, and Header. Breaking changes: Button now requires a variant prop (primary/secondary/ghost); the old color prop is removed. Grace estimated 3 days of frontend work. Deadline: integration complete by April 22nd so QA can test before the April 30th sprint end. Carol to stay on call for design questions.

Slack #platform — Frank Liu: iOS app v2.3.1 is live in the App Store. Key changes: new auth flow using the v2 token endpoint, improved offline mode with local SQLite cache, and fixed a crash when opening notifications from background state. Known issue: push notification badge count is sometimes off by one when the app comes back from background — root cause identified (race condition in the background refresh handler), fix targeted for v2.3.2 next week. Frank owns the fix; David Park will help with the APNs side if needed.

GDPR audit prep — Alice Chen to all. The GDPR compliance audit is scheduled for June 15th with the external auditor Schneider & Associates. We need to complete three deliverables by June 1st: (1) Data flow diagram covering all user data paths from ingestion through deletion — owner: Bob Rivera. (2) Retention policy documentation for each data category — owner: Alice Chen. (3) Evidence of data subject request (DSR) fulfillment within 72 hours for the past 6 months — owner: Grace Wong. Henry Adams will provide the audit trail exports from the system.

Slack #web — Grace Wong: migrated the dashboard to the new auth token format. Deployed to production 3:15pm UTC. All web sessions now use the structured error response from the v2 auth service. Tested: login, logout, token refresh, expired session handling, and account switching. No regressions in automated tests. One manual test still pending: the SSO flow with Okta — scheduled for tomorrow morning with the identity team. Grace will post results in this channel.

Architecture review — API v2 design. Bob Rivera presented the proposed changes for the v2 API. Key decisions made: (1) All endpoints return a consistent envelope: {data, meta, error}. (2) Pagination uses cursor-based tokens, not page numbers — max page size 200. (3) Rate limiting: 100 req/min for read endpoints, 20 req/min for write endpoints, applied per API key. (4) Breaking change: all timestamps are returned in UTC ISO 8601 with millisecond precision. (5) Deprecation policy: v1 endpoints will be supported for 90 days after v2 GA. Alice Chen approved the design; implementation begins Monday.

Slack #qa — Henry Adams: the flaky search integration tests are now fixed. Root cause was a test isolation issue: tests were sharing a worker instance and one test left background jobs in the queue that interfered with subsequent tests. Fix: each test now gets a dedicated in-process worker with a fresh Redis namespace. All 47 search tests passing 10/10 consecutive runs in CI. The auth token refresh flakes are still under investigation — tentative fix by Wednesday.

Document: Incident post-mortem — Search outage April 10th. Duration: 42 minutes (14:23–15:05 UTC). Impact: search and ask endpoints returned 503 for all users. Root cause: the database connection pool was exhausted due to a connection leak in the new query planner introduced in PR #1482. Detection: on-call alert fired at 14:25 (p95 latency crossed 5s threshold). Mitigation: rolled back PR #1482 at 15:02. Fix: added connection pool monitoring and a max_connection_lifetime to the SQLAlchemy pool config. Owner of fix: Eva Sharma. Preventive: connection pool metrics now in the runbook.

Sprint planning — April 15 sprint. Commitments for the two-week sprint: (1) Eva Sharma: search query planner fix (P0), connection pool monitoring (P0). (2) Grace Wong: complete SSO flow validation (P1), start GDPR data flow diagram (P2). (3) Frank Liu: fix push notification badge count race condition (P1). (4) Henry Adams: fix auth token refresh flakes (P1), increase QA automation to 82% (P2). (5) David Park: complete Postgres 16 upgrade runbook (P1). Velocity last sprint: 47 points. This sprint estimate: 52 points (stretch). Alice Chen is sprint lead.

Slack #backend — Bob Rivera: API v2 development branch is created: feat/api-v2. All v2 work goes into this branch. PR review process: at least two approvals from Bob, Eva, or Grace before merge. Do not merge directly to main — v2 will land as one big branch merge after integration testing. Bob is the merge lead. If you find v1 bugs, fix them on main directly. Eva Sharma asked about the timeline for the v1 deprecation notice to customers; Alice Chen will draft the notice by April 25th.

Document: Runbook — Postgres 16 upgrade. Pre-checks: (1) Verify all application connections use connection pooling (PgBouncer or SQLAlchemy pool). (2) Take a full database backup and verify restore in staging. (3) Test the HNSW index rebuild on a staging snapshot — estimated time 90 minutes on 50GB dataset. Upgrade steps: (1) Enable maintenance mode in Caddy (returns 503 for all traffic). (2) pg_upgrade from v15 to v16. (3) Rebuild all GIN and HNSW indexes. (4) Run ANALYZE on critical tables. (5) Disable maintenance mode. Post-checks: verify all health checks green, run smoke tests, spot-check query plans. Owner: David Park.

Slack #design — Carol Kim: the brand refresh is approved by leadership! Final palette: primary #1A56DB (deep blue), secondary #7E3AF2 (purple), success #057A55 (green), warning #E3A008 (amber), error #C81E1E (red). Typography: Inter for UI, Jetbrains Mono for code. Carol will update the Figma design system today and publish the component spec doc. Grace Wong and Frank Liu both acknowledged. Carol set a deadline of April 20th for all product teams to adopt the new components in their feature branches.

1:1 — Bob Rivera and Eva Sharma. Eva raised a concern about the connection pool sizing on production: current max_connections=20 per app instance, 3 app instances, shared Postgres max_connections=60. During the April 10th incident, pool was exhausted at peak load. Proposed: increase max_connections per instance to 10 (to stay under Postgres limit), add pgBouncer transaction pooling in front of Postgres, set max_connection_lifetime=300s to prevent stale connections. Bob approved the pgBouncer addition; David Park to implement by April 30th.

Slack #platform — David Park: monitoring stack update. Added three new dashboards in Grafana: (1) Connection pool saturation per service. (2) Redis memory usage and eviction rate. (3) API error rate by endpoint and workspace tier. Alerting: PagerDuty integration updated with new rules — connection pool > 80% triggers P2 alert, > 95% triggers P1. All on-call engineers should review the updated runbook. Alice Chen flagged that the GDPR audit may require evidence of monitoring — David will export the Grafana dashboard configs to the compliance evidence folder.

Retrospective — April sprint. Went well: search performance improvement (Eva); iOS v2.3.1 shipped on time (Frank); design handoff was smooth (Carol, Grace). To improve: incident response time needs a clearer escalation path — Henry suggests a decision tree doc. Bob Rivera to own the escalation runbook. Not great: GDPR prep is behind schedule; the original owner of the data flow diagram was unclear. Alice Chen reassigned it explicitly to Grace Wong with a hard deadline of May 25th. Team agreed to add GDPR prep to the weekly all-hands status check starting next week.
